message.content:
You are testing ONE hypothesis for defect D65 (wanbond WAN-bonding tunnel throughput investigation). Return the structured JSON evidence block per your role contract. READ-ONLY.

**hypothesisId:** H7

**statement (verbatim):** H-D: Bufferbloat — an oversized/unbounded internal TX (or reorder) queue with drop-tail and no AQM/BDP bound; the ~1s loaded RTT (idle 40ms) + 13% UDP loss at only 8Mbps offered is the classic oversized-buffer + tail-drop signature

**branchContext:** Defect D65: UDP offered 8Mbps → 6.9 delivered at 13% loss with the queue building to ~1s loaded RTT (idle ~40ms). Pi4→Starlink→o3. One of 5 disjoint roots. Confirm or rule out that an internal send-side or resequencer queue is sized far above the path BDP (~40ms × link rate) with plain drop-tail and no AQM/CoDel/pacing, so under load it accumulates ~1s of standing queue then tail-drops ~13%. This is a queue-management defect independent of the rate ceiling's cause. Determine: (1) every bounded/unbounded queue on the datapath (send channels, per-path queues, reorder buffer) and its capacity units (packets/bytes/time); (2) the drop policy when full (tail-drop vs AQM); (3) whether any pacing/backpressure exists. Report SUPPORTS and CONTRADICTS evidence.

**leads:**
- channel/queue construction in internal/bind (grep `make(chan` and slice-backed queues)
- internal/reseq/reseq.go (reorder buffer depth & drop policy)
- internal/sched (per-path queues)
- grep for codel/aqm/pace/backpressure/highWater/watermark across internal/

Emit the ### Session summary block then the fenced json block as the last content.

Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".

Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. input.query: reorder buffer reseq depth drop policy, per-path send queue channels, TX queue capacity, pacing backpressure watermark

content.text: Grep/Glob aren't available as tools. Let me read the reseq resequencer struct/window sizing and the send path directly.

content.content:
1	package reseq
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injected time source. Production wires a monotonic wall clock
10	// (SystemClock); tests wire a hand-advanced fake so ordering, window, and
11	// timeout behaviour are deterministic with no real sleeps. It is intentionally
12	// structurally identical to telemetry.Clock so the same fake can drive both.
13	type Clock interface {
14		Now() time.Time
15	}
16	
17	// SystemClock is the production Clock backed by the monotonic wall clock.
18	type SystemClock struct{}
19	
20	// Now returns the current monotonic time.
21	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
22	
23	// Discontinuity/resync guard tuning. A DATA frame is UNAUTHENTICATED by design
24	// (internal/frame/frame.go: "DATA/PARITY forgeable by design"), so a garbage
25	// datagram decoding as KindData (p ~ 1/256) delivers a uniformly-random uint64
26	// OuterSeq, and a genuine peer PROCESS RESTART resets the peer's outerSeq counter
27	// to 1. Neither may be trusted to move the release point arbitrarily.
28	const (
29		// resyncFactor (K) bounds how far AHEAD of the release point a single
30		// unauthenticated frame is trusted to advance the window as a plausible loss
31		// burst. Under bounded cross-path reorder a legitimate frame sits within one
32		// window of next; a forward jump up to K windows is still treated as a loss
33		// burst (the sound bounded-memory window-advance handles it in O(window)), but
34		// a jump of K windows or more is SUSPECT: a single such frame never advances
35		// next. K=4 leaves generous headroom above the 1-window reorder bound while
36		// capping the release point a single trusted frame can skip to (K-1) windows.
37		resyncFactor = 4
38	
39		// resyncCorroborate (C) is how many out-of-band frames carrying DISTINCT seqs
40		// that mutually span less than one window must be observed before the release
41		// point is re-pinned. A genuine peer restart (1,2,3,...) or a consistent
42		// long-outage forward jump emits connected, distinct seqs that trivially
43		// corroborate. A repeated identical seq does NOT advance the count: a single
44		// junk or forged datagram re-delivered — a network duplicate, or a forger
45		// replaying one datagram — contributes only ONE distinct seq, so it can never
46		// self-corroborate. Corroboration therefore requires C INDEPENDENT junk seqs to
47		// land mutually within one window; each is independent in 2^64, so that occurs
48		// with probability ~(window/2^64)^(C-1) ~ 1e-32 for C=3 and window=2048 — junk
49		// never triggers a resync, while a real discontinuity resyncs after losing at
50		// most C-1 frames. Any in-band (near-current) frame resets the run, so ordinary
51		// traffic interspersed with rare junk never accumulates a false corroboration.
52		resyncCorroborate = 3
53	)
54	
55	// Item is one released inner datagram plus the outer source address of the DATA
56	// frame that carried it. The source travels with the payload so the multipath
57	// Bind can pin its single virtual endpoint to the FIRST delivered frame's
58	// source even when that frame was buffered and released out of arrival order.
59	type Item struct {
60		Payload []byte
61		Src     netip.AddrPort
62	}
63	
64	// slot is one ring cell. A live cell (occupied) holds exactly one buffered DATA
65	// frame whose OuterSeq maps to this index; seq is retained so a stale cell (an
66	// already-drained seq that shares the index modulo window) is never mistaken for
67	// a live one.
68	type slot struct {
69		seq      uint64
70		src      netip.AddrPort
71		payload  []byte
72		occupied bool
73	}
74	
75	// Resequencer is a bounded-window, timeout-based receive resequencing buffer. It
76	// consumes DATA frames tagged with the multipath codec's OWN outer sequence
77	// number (frame.Data.OuterSeq — never WireGuard's inner counter) arriving out of
78	// order across paths, and releases the decoded inner datagrams in strictly
79	// ascending outer-seq order before they reach the WireGuard engine. This absorbs
80	// multipath reorder entirely in the outer layer so WG's RFC 6479 anti-replay
81	// filter (window 8128, see docs/p0-findings.md §6) only ever sees monotonically
82	// increasing inner counters and never drops a legitimately-delivered packet.
83	//
84	// Guarantees (see the package tests):
85	//   - Ordering: released frames are strictly ascending in outer-seq. A frame
86	//     whose seq is below the release point is dropped, never delivered late, so
87	//     delivery is monotonic and WG sees no replay-window regression.
88	//   - Exactly-once: duplicates (same seq re-observed before or after release) are
89	//     dropped; every deliverable frame is released exactly once.
90	//   - Bounded memory: at most `window` frames are buffered. A frame at or beyond
91	//     next+window forces the window to advance (releasing/skipping the tail),
92	//     so an adversarial reorder/loss trace cannot grow the buffer without bound.
93	//   - Timeout progress: when the head-of-line seq is missing, buffered frames
94	//     ahead of the gap are held at most `timeout`; then the gap is skipped
95	//     (treated as lost) and the run released, rather than stalling forever. Each
96	//     distinct head-of-line gap gets its OWN full timeout.
97	//   - Bounded compute: every Observe runs in O(window). No frame — however wild
98	//     its (unauthenticated, possibly forged) seq — can make the release point
99	//     advance one seq at a time across a gap of up to 2^64 under the mutex.
100	//   - Discontinuity resilience: an unauthenticated frame whose seq lies farther
101	//     than resyncFactor*window from next never moves the release point on its own.
102	//     A genuine peer restart or long outage — a run of frames carrying DISTINCT
103	//     seqs that mutually fall within one window — re-pins (resyncs) the release
104	//     point within a bounded number of frames; uniformly-random junk seqs, and a
105	//     single junk/forged seq re-delivered any number of times, do not.
106	//
107	// Concurrency: every method is guarded by one mutex, so the shared instance is
108	// safe for the multipath Bind's per-path receive goroutines. The mutex is held
109	// only for in-memory bookkeeping — never across a syscall — so it does not
110	// perturb the Bind's lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path or its
111	// syscall-outside-mutex send discipline.
112	//
113	// FEC seam (T24): Observe is the sole ingestion point and is keyed purely by
114	// outer-seq. An FEC decoder inserted on the receive path recovers missing DATA
115	// frames from PARITY and feeds each recovered frame's ORIGINAL outer-seq through
116	// Observe, identically to a natively-received frame — so FEC recovery slots in
117	// BEFORE resequencing with no change to this type.
118	type Resequencer struct {
119		window  uint64
120		timeout time.Duration
121		clock   Clock
122	
123		mu      sync.Mutex
124		started bool   // false until the first Observe pins the initial next
125		next    uint64 // lowest outer-seq not yet released
126		ring    []slot // len == window; indexed by seq % window
127		buf     int    // number of occupied slots
128	
129		ready    []Item // FIFO of released items awaiting Pop
130		readyPos int    // read cursor into ready (front of the FIFO)
131	
132		// Head-of-line timeout: when next is a gap but frames ahead are buffered,
133		// waiting is armed with a deadline measured from when the gap first formed.
134		waiting  bool
135		deadline time.Time
136	
137		// Discontinuity/resync guard: the current run of out-of-band (suspect) frames.
138		// resyncSeqs holds the DISTINCT suspect seqs seen in the run (a repeated
139		// identical seq does not advance corroboration); [resyncLo, resyncHi] is the
140		// run's seq span. When resyncSeqs reaches resyncCorroborate distinct seqs the
141		// run is deemed a real discontinuity and the release point re-pins (see
142		// tryResync). It holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so memory stays bounded.
143		resyncSeqs []uint64
144		resyncLo   uint64
145		resyncHi   uint64
146	
147		// Diagnostics (read via the accessors; useful for the bounded-memory asserts).
148		highWater   int    // max occupied slots ever held
149		dropDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
150		dropLate    uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
151		dropSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
152		skipped     uint64 // seqs skipped (treated lost) by window-advance or timeout
153		releasedN   uint64 // frames released for delivery
154		resyncs     uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
155		rebaselines uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
156	}
157	
158	// New returns a resequencer buffering at most window outer-seq positions and
159	// holding a head-of-line-blocked run for at most timeout before skipping the gap.
160	// window must be positive; timeout may be zero (a missing head seq is skipped as
161	// soon as the next Observe/Pop advances the clock past the arrival instant).
162	func New(window uint64, timeout time.Duration, clock Clock) *Resequencer {
163		if window == 0 {
164			panic("reseq: window must be positive")
165		}
166		if clock == nil {
167			panic("reseq: clock must be non-nil")
168		}
169		return &Resequencer{
170			window:  window,
171			timeout: timeout,
172			clock:   clock,
173			ring:    make([]slot, window),
174		}
175	}
176	
177	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
178	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
179	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
180	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
181	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
182	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
183		now := r.clock.Now()
184		r.mu.Lock()
185		defer r.mu.Unlock()
186	
187		if !r.started {
188			r.started = true
189			r.next = seq
190		}
191	
192		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
193		// where this frame lands.
194		r.expire(now)
195	
196		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
197		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
198		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
199		if !r.admit(seq) {
200			return
201		}
202	
203		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
204		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
205			r.dropDup++
206			return
207		}
208		cell.seq = seq
209		cell.src = src
210		cell.payload = payload
211		cell.occupied = true
212		r.buf++
213		if r.buf > r.highWater {
214			r.highWater = r.buf
215		}
216	
217		r.drain()
218		r.arm(now)
219	}
220	
221	// ObserveRecovered ingests a payload the FEC decoder reconstructed from parity (T24).
222	// Unlike Observe it NEVER moves or re-pins the release point: a recovered frame is a
223	// REPAIR for a past gap, so if it has already fallen below the release point it is
224	// dropped as too-late, and it can neither window-advance nor corroborate a resync — so
225	// FEC recovery can never CAUSE the burst loss it exists to prevent (a late batch of K
226	// recovered seqs below `next` must not be able to dump the live buffer via a backward
227	// resync). It returns whether the frame was PLACED (landed at or above the release
228	// point and will be delivered in order); a dropped-late recovered frame returns false,
229	// which the datapath uses to keep the /metrics recovered counter honest — counting
230	// frames actually delivered ahead of the release point, not merely reconstructed.
231	func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) bool {
232		now := r.clock.Now()
233		r.mu.Lock()
234		defer r.mu.Unlock()
235	
236		if !r.started {
237			r.started = true
238			r.next = seq
239		}
240		r.expire(now)
241	
242		// The placement window is EXACTLY [next, next+window). A recovered frame below next
243		// is too late (drop, and — crucially — never feed the resync guard). One at or beyond
244		// next+window would require a window-advance we refuse to perform for a reconstructed
245		// frame, since that would skip live seqs on the strength of a repair. Neither branch
246		// touches next or the corroboration run.
247		if seq < r.next {
248			if r.next-seq > r.window {
249				r.dropSuspect++
250			} else {
251				r.dropLate++
252			}
253			return false
254		}

279	// admit classifies seq against the release window and the discontinuity guard and
280	// decides whether the frame should be buffered. It returns true when seq now lies
281	// inside [next, next+window) and should be placed; false (having bumped the
282	// appropriate drop counter) when the frame is dropped. It may advance next by a
283	// bounded amount on a plausible loss burst, or re-pin next on a corroborated
284	// discontinuity. Caller holds r.mu.
285	func (r *Resequencer) admit(seq uint64) bool {
286		if seq < r.next {
287			// Below the release point. A frame more than one window below next is
288			// impossibly late under bounded reorder — treat it as SUSPECT (a peer
289			// restart resets outerSeq to 1, so its frames land here). A frame within a
290			// window below next is an ordinary straggler/duplicate.
291			if r.next-seq > r.window {
292				if r.tryResync(seq) {
293					return true
294				}
295				r.dropSuspect++
296				return false
297			}
298			r.resyncReset() // near-current traffic: not a discontinuity
299			r.dropLate++
300			return false

74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()

84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)

88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes

91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {

94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,

105				})

138		// resyncSeqs holds the DISTINCT suspect seqs seen in the run (a repeated
139		// identical seq does not advance corroboration); [resyncLo, resyncHi] is the
140		// run's seq span. When resyncSeqs reaches resyncCorroborate distinct seqs the
141		// run is deemed a real discontinuity and the release point re-pins (see
142		// tryResync). It holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so memory stays bounded.
143		resyncSeqs []uint64
144		resyncLo   uint64

150		dropLate    uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
151		dropSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
152		skipped     uint64 // seqs skipped (treated lost) by window-advance or timeout

155		rebaselines uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
156	}

158	// New returns a resequencer buffering at most window outer-seq positions and
159	// holding a head-of-line-blocked run for at most timeout before skipping the gap.
160	// window must be positive; timeout may be zero (a missing head seq is skipped as
161	// soon as the next Observe/Pop advances the clock past the arrival instant).
162	func New(window uint64, timeout time.Duration, clock Clock) *Resequencer {
163		if window == 0 {
164			panic("reseq: window must be positive")
165		}
166		if clock == nil {
167			panic("reseq: clock must be non-nil")
168		}
169		return &Resequencer{

177	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
178	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
179	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
180	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
181	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
182	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
183		now := r.clock.Now()
184		r.mu.Lock()
185		defer r.mu.Unlock()
186	
187		if !r.started {
188			r.started = true
189			r.next = seq
190		}
191	
192		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
193		// where this frame lands.
194		r.expire(now)
195	
196		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
197		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
198		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
199		if !r.admit(seq) {
200			return
201		}
202	
203		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
204		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
205			r.dropDup++
206			return
207		}
208		cell.seq = seq
209		cell.src = src
210		cell.payload = payload
211		cell.occupied = true
212		r.buf++
213		if r.buf > r.highWater {
214			r.highWater = r.buf
215		}
216	
217		r.drain()
218		r.arm(now)
219	}

13	// satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in the adapter's unit test, so the mapping and rate
14	// derivation are testable without a running engine. PeerSnapshots generalizes the
15	// pre-T94 PathSnapshots/FECSnapshot (primary-peer-only) to every bound peer, so a
16	// single-peer edge/hub still maps 1:1 onto today's series (len(PeerSnapshots())==1) and
17	// a multi-peer concentrator's additional peers surface as additional entries.

19		PeerSnapshots() []bind.PeerSnapshot
20	}
21	
22	// sampleKey identifies one (peer,path) pair in the throughput last-sample map (T94): a
23	// path name alone is only unique WITHIN one peer, not across peers (each peer's path
24	// set is independently configured/named in principle), so keying by path name alone
25	// would let two peers' samples for a same-named path clobber each other's rate
26	// derivation. peer is "" for the single-peer edge/hub and a concentrator's primary,
27	// exactly matching PeerSnapshot.Name — so a single-peer config's map degenerates to the
28	// pre-T94 keying (peer is always "", so the key varies only by path).
29	type sampleKey struct {
30		peer, path string

33	// metricsSource adapts the multipath Bind onto metrics.Source: at every scrape it
34	// reads the Bind's cumulative per-(peer,path) byte counters + telemetry, and DERIVES
35	// the per-(peer,path) throughput as the rate of the (tx+rx) byte-counter delta since
36	// the previous scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the
37	// Bind's hot path a pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the
38	// only stateful, scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a
39	// (peer,path) reports zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move
40	// backwards (only possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that
41	// interval rather than a spurious negative.
42	type metricsSource struct {
43		provider trafficProvider
44		// session yields the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2), read from the
45		// engine at scrape time. It is separate from provider because the bind stays
46		// WG-unaware — the session signal is sourced by the device layer's engine seam.
47		session sessionSnapshotter
48		clock   telemetry.Clock
49	
50		mu   sync.Mutex
51		last map[sampleKey]byteSample
52	}
53	
54	// byteSample is the previous scrape's cumulative (tx+rx) total for one (peer,path) and
55	// the instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.

58		atNanos int64
59	}
60	
61	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind (per-peer path traffic, FEC,
62	// and resequencer counters) and the engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The
63	// clock is injected so the throughput derivation is deterministic under test.
64	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
65		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[sampleKey]byteSample)}
66	}

68	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
69	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-
70	// (peer,path) last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PeerSnapshots() reads
71	// atomics and each prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock
72	// across a prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
73	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
75		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
76	
77		total := 0
78		for _, peer := range peers {
79			total += len(peer.Paths)
80		}
81	
82		s.mu.Lock()
83		defer s.mu.Unlock()
84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)
85		for _, peer := range peers {
86			for _, t := range peer.Paths {
87				key := sampleKey{peer: peer.Name, path: t.Name}
88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
89				var tput float64
90				if prev, ok := s.last[key]; ok {
91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}

112	// counters (T24, T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer. Unlike the
113	// per-path throughput, the FEC counters are cumulative and need no rate derivation or
114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
115	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() []metrics.FECSnapshot {
116		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()

119			f := peer.FEC
120			out[i] = metrics.FECSnapshot{

122				DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,
123				RepairPackets:        f.ParityFrames,

125				UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
126				DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,

128				ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
129			}

138		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
139		out := make([]metrics.ReseqSnapshot, len(peers))
140		for i, peer := range peers {
141			out[i] = metrics.ReseqSnapshot{Peer: peer.Name, Stats: peer.Reseq}
142		}

154		}

15	// tunDiagnosticInterval is the minimum spacing between actionable EIO diagnostics for a
16	// single TUN. An interface left DOWN (or otherwise wedged) fails EVERY subsequent write, so
17	// without a rate limit a write storm would emit one diagnostic record per dropped packet.
18	// The raw amneziawg-go engine error ("Failed to write packets to TUN device: input/output
19	// error", logged unconditionally via engineLogger's Errorf) still surfaces on every failure
20	// underneath — this decorator adds ONE extra, actionable record per interval on top of that,
21	// it never suppresses the engine's own logging.
22	const tunDiagnosticInterval = 30 * time.Second
23	
24	// diagnosingTUN wraps a tun.Device and, on a Write that fails with EIO, emits ONE rate-limited
25	// actionable ERROR naming the probable cause (the interface's link state and MTU) and the
26	// remedy, alongside the raw error for debugging (D39, I3). Left undiagnosed, a write to a DOWN
27	// wanbond0 surfaces only as the engine's generic "input/output error" — accurate but useless to
28	// an operator who has never hit the failure mode before. Every OTHER tun.Device method passes
29	// straight through via the embedded interface; only Write is intercepted.
30	type diagnosingTUN struct {
31		tun.Device
32		name string
33		log  log.Logger
34		// now and probeState are seams for tests: production wires time.Now and ifState (the real
35		// SIOCGIFFLAGS/SIOCGIFMTU inspection); a test injects a fake clock and a scripted interface
36		// state so it can assert the DOWN/UP wording without a real, privileged network interface.
37		now        func() time.Time
38		probeState func(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error)
39	
40		mu       sync.Mutex
41		lastDiag time.Time
42	}
43	
44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}
49	
50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}
60	
61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}

743	// session id never conflates two peers' probe streams, and a runtime-added path (T30) reuses it
744	// so its probes join this boot's stream and the peer's reflector adopts them without a
745	// challenge reset.
746	func buildScheduler(cfg *config.Config, psk config.Key, sessionID uint64, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, []*telemetry.Prober, bind.ProberFactory, error) {
747		clock := telemetry.SystemClock{}
748		proberCfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
749			LossWindow: telemetry.DefaultLossWindow,
750			Liveness: telemetry.LivenessConfig{
751				DownAfter:        telemetry.DefaultDownAfter,
752				UpAfterSuccesses: telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses,
753			},
754		}
755		// newProber mints one path's Prober with the shared session/config/clock, keyed on THIS
756		// peer's psk. It is the single construction point for this peer's boot-time AND runtime
757		// paths, so both measure liveness identically.
758		newProber := func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
759			return telemetry.NewProber(name, id, sessionID, psk, proberCfg, clock, lg)
760		}
761		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(cfg.Paths))
762		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(cfg.Paths))
763		quality := make([]sched.PathQuality, len(cfg.Paths))
764		for i := range cfg.Paths {
765			probers[i] = newProber(cfg.Paths[i].Name, uint8(i))
766			health[i] = probers[i]
767			quality[i] = probers[i]
768		}
769		// Policy is a config choice: active-backup (P1, default) or the T21 weighted-
770		// aggregation policy. Both consume the SAME per-path *Prober set — a *Prober
771		// satisfies BOTH PathHealth (liveness) and PathQuality (RTT/loss Estimate) — so the
772		// probe transport drives the very liveness/quality the scheduler selects on, and the
773		// swap is behind config with no Bind change.
774		scheduler, err := selectScheduler(cfg, health, quality, clock, lg)
775		if err != nil {
776			return nil, nil, nil, err
777		}
778		return scheduler, probers, newProber, nil
779	}
780	
781	// selectScheduler builds the send scheduler the configured policy names, over the
782	// per-path health (and, for the weighted policy, quality) sources. active-backup is
783	// the P1 default; weighted is T21. The weighted knobs are validated at config load,
784	// so translating them here cannot fail on range — only NewWeighted's structural
785	// checks (which the wiring satisfies) apply.
786	func selectScheduler(cfg *config.Config, health []sched.PathHealth, quality []sched.PathQuality, clock telemetry.Clock, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, error) {
787		switch cfg.Scheduler.Policy {
788		case config.PolicyWeighted:
789			sc := cfg.Scheduler
790			return sched.NewWeighted(health, quality, sched.WeightedConfig{
791				PerPathCapacity:   sc.PerPathCapacityFPS,
792				EngageFraction:    sc.EngageFraction,
793				DisengageFraction: sc.DisengageFraction,
794				CollapseDwell:     sc.CollapseDwell,
795				LoadTau:           sc.LoadTau,
796				Pacing:            sc.PacingEnabled,
797				PacingBurst:       sc.PacingBurstFrames,
798				WeightRTTFloor:    sc.WeightRTTFloor,
799				WeightLossFloor:   sc.WeightLossFloor,
800			}, clock, lg)
801		default:
802			// active-backup (and the empty default, normalized to it at config load).
803			return sched.NewActiveBackup(
804				health,
805				sched.Config{FailbackAfter: defaultFailbackDwell},
806				clock,
807				lg,
808			)
809		}
810	}

44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}

50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}

61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}

743	// session id never conflates two peers' probe streams, and a runtime-added path (T30) reuses it
744	// so its probes join this boot's stream and the peer's reflector adopts them without a
745	// challenge reset.
746	func buildScheduler(cfg *config.Config, psk config.Key, sessionID uint64, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, []*telemetry.Prober, bind.ProberFactory, error) {
747		clock := telemetry.SystemClock{}
748		proberCfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
749			LossWindow: telemetry.DefaultLossWindow,
750			Liveness: telemetry.LivenessConfig{
751				DownAfter:        telemetry.DefaultDownAfter,
752				UpAfterSuccesses: telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses,
753			},
754		}
755		// newProber mints one path's Prober with the shared session/config/clock, keyed on THIS
756		// peer's psk. It is the single construction point for this peer's boot-time AND runtime
757		// paths, so both measure liveness identically.
758		newProber := func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
759			return telemetry.NewProber(name, id, sessionID, psk, proberCfg, clock, lg)
760		}
761		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(cfg.Paths))
762		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(cfg.Paths))
763		quality := make([]sched.PathQuality, len(cfg.Paths))
764		for i := range cfg.Paths {
765			probers[i] = newProber(cfg.Paths[i].Name, uint8(i))
766			health[i] = probers[i]
767			quality[i] = probers[i]
768		}
769		// Policy is a config choice: active-backup (P1, default) or the T21 weighted-
770		// aggregation policy. Both consume the SAME per-path *Prober set — a *Prober
771		// satisfies BOTH PathHealth (liveness) and PathQuality (RTT/loss Estimate) — so the
772		// probe transport drives the very liveness/quality the scheduler selects on, and the
773		// swap is behind config with no Bind change.
774		scheduler, err := selectScheduler(cfg, health, quality, clock, lg)
775		if err != nil {
776			return nil, nil, nil, err
777		}
778		return scheduler, probers, newProber, nil
779	}
780	
781	// selectScheduler builds the send scheduler the configured policy names, over the
782	// per-path health (and, for the weighted policy, quality) sources. active-backup is
783	// the P1 default; weighted is T21. The weighted knobs are validated at config load,
784	// so translating them here cannot fail on range — only NewWeighted's structural
785	// checks (which the wiring satisfies) apply.
786	func selectScheduler(cfg *config.Config, health []sched.PathHealth, quality []sched.PathQuality, clock telemetry.Clock, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, error) {
787		switch cfg.Scheduler.Policy {
788		case config.PolicyWeighted:
789			sc := cfg.Scheduler
790			return sched.NewWeighted(health, quality, sched.WeightedConfig{
791				PerPathCapacity:   sc.PerPathCapacityFPS,
792				EngageFraction:    sc.EngageFraction,
793				DisengageFraction: sc.DisengageFraction,
794				CollapseDwell:     sc.CollapseDwell,
795				LoadTau:           sc.LoadTau,
796				Pacing:            sc.PacingEnabled,
797				PacingBurst:       sc.PacingBurstFrames,
798				WeightRTTFloor:    sc.WeightRTTFloor,
799				WeightLossFloor:   sc.WeightLossFloor,
800			}, clock, lg)
801		default:
802			// active-backup (and the empty default, normalized to it at config load).
803			return sched.NewActiveBackup(
804				health,
805				sched.Config{FailbackAfter: defaultFailbackDwell},
806				clock,
807				lg,
808			)
809		}
810	}

477	// Path is one physical WAN uplink. The edge binds each path's UDP socket to
478	// SourceAddr so the upstream router pins it to the intended WAN; the
479	// concentrator learns the real per-path endpoints from authenticated traffic.
480	type Path struct {
481		// Name is a stable human-readable identifier for the path (e.g. "starlink").
482		Name string `toml:"name"`
483		// SourceAddr is the local source IP the path's socket binds to.
484		SourceAddr netip.Addr `toml:"-"`
485		// SourceAddrRaw is the TOML string form of SourceAddr; parsed in normalize.
486		SourceAddrRaw string `toml:"source_addr"`
487		// DestAddr is the OPTIONAL per-path concentrator endpoint ("ip:port") the
488		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
489		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
490		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
491		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
492		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.
493		DestAddr netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
494		// DestAddrRaw is the TOML string form of DestAddr; parsed in normalize.
495		DestAddrRaw string `toml:"dest_addr"`
496		// LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec is the OPERATOR-DECLARED bottleneck bandwidth of this
497		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to
498		// size the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the bandwidth-delay product
499		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
500		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.
501		// Zero means "not declared" (the synthetic default pace is kept).
502		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
503		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.
504		// "50Mbit" / "1Gbit" / "500kbit" (SI bit/s units; the "bit" suffix may be written
505		// "bps"). Parsed in normalize; a non-positive or unparseable value fails fast.
506		LinkBandwidthRaw string `toml:"link_bandwidth"`
507		// LinkRTT is the OPERATOR-DECLARED baseline RTT of this uplink, parsed from
508		// LinkRTTRaw in normalize. It is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product pace
509		// burst (one RTT of in-flight frames); required (> 0) when LinkBandwidth is set and
510		// pacing is enabled under the weighted policy, ignored otherwise.
511		LinkRTT time.Duration `toml:"-"`
512		// LinkRTTRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LinkRTT, e.g. "45ms". Parsed in
513		// normalize; an unparseable or non-positive value fails fast.
514		LinkRTTRaw string `toml:"link_rtt"`

11	// trafficProvider is the read seam the metrics adapter consumes: the multipath Bind's
12	// per-PEER traffic+telemetry, FEC, and resequencer snapshot (T93/T94). *bind.Multipath
13	// satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in the adapter's unit test, so the mapping and rate
14	// derivation are testable without a running engine. PeerSnapshots generalizes the
15	// pre-T94 PathSnapshots/FECSnapshot (primary-peer-only) to every bound peer, so a
16	// single-peer edge/hub still maps 1:1 onto today's series (len(PeerSnapshots())==1) and
17	// a multi-peer concentrator's additional peers surface as additional entries.
18	type trafficProvider interface {
19		PeerSnapshots() []bind.PeerSnapshot
20	}
21	
22	// sampleKey identifies one (peer,path) pair in the throughput last-sample map (T94): a
23	// path name alone is only unique WITHIN one peer, not across peers (each peer's path
24	// set is independently configured/named in principle), so keying by path name alone
25	// would let two peers' samples for a same-named path clobber each other's rate
26	// derivation. peer is "" for the single-peer edge/hub and a concentrator's primary,
27	// exactly matching PeerSnapshot.Name — so a single-peer config's map degenerates to the
28	// pre-T94 keying (peer is always "", so the key varies only by path).
29	type sampleKey struct {
30		peer, path string
31	}
32	
33	// metricsSource adapts the multipath Bind onto metrics.Source: at every scrape it
34	// reads the Bind's cumulative per-(peer,path) byte counters + telemetry, and DERIVES
35	// the per-(peer,path) throughput as the rate of the (tx+rx) byte-counter delta since
36	// the previous scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the
37	// Bind's hot path a pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the
38	// only stateful, scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a
39	// (peer,path) reports zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move
40	// backwards (only possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that
41	// interval rather than a spurious negative.
42	type metricsSource struct {
43		provider trafficProvider
44		// session yields the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2), read from the
45		// engine at scrape time. It is separate from provider because the bind stays
46		// WG-unaware — the session signal is sourced by the device layer's engine seam.
47		session sessionSnapshotter
48		clock   telemetry.Clock
49	
50		mu   sync.Mutex
51		last map[sampleKey]byteSample
52	}
53	
54	// byteSample is the previous scrape's cumulative (tx+rx) total for one (peer,path) and
55	// the instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.
56	type byteSample struct {
57		total   uint64
58		atNanos int64
59	}
60	
61	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind (per-peer path traffic, FEC,
62	// and resequencer counters) and the engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The
63	// clock is injected so the throughput derivation is deterministic under test.
64	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
65		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[sampleKey]byteSample)}
66	}
67	
68	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
69	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-
70	// (peer,path) last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PeerSnapshots() reads
71	// atomics and each prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock
72	// across a prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
73	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
75		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
76	
77		total := 0
78		for _, peer := range peers {
79			total += len(peer.Paths)
80		}
81	
82		s.mu.Lock()
83		defer s.mu.Unlock()
84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)
85		for _, peer := range peers {
86			for _, t := range peer.Paths {
87				key := sampleKey{peer: peer.Name, path: t.Name}
88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
89				var tput float64
90				if prev, ok := s.last[key]; ok {
91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}
110	
111	// FEC implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer connection-scoped FEC
112	// counters (T24, T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer. Unlike the
113	// per-path throughput, the FEC counters are cumulative and need no rate derivation or
114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
115	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() []metrics.FECSnapshot {
116		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
117		out := make([]metrics.FECSnapshot, len(peers))
118		for i, peer := range peers {
119			f := peer.FEC
120			out[i] = metrics.FECSnapshot{
121				Peer:                 peer.Name,
122				DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,
123				RepairPackets:        f.ParityFrames,
124				RecoveredPackets:     f.Recovered,
125				UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
126				DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
127				RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
128				ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
129			}
130		}
131		return out
132	}
133	
134	// Reseq implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer resequencer counters
135	// (T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer — a direct pass-through of
136	// the resequencer's own cumulative Stats(), like FEC needing no rate derivation.
137	func (s *metricsSource) Reseq() []metrics.ReseqSnapshot {
138		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
139		out := make([]metrics.ReseqSnapshot, len(peers))
140		for i, peer := range peers {
141			out[i] = metrics.ReseqSnapshot{Peer: peer.Name, Stats: peer.Reseq}
142		}
143		return out
144	}
145	
146	// PeerNames implements metrics.Source: it returns the current bound-peer name set,
147	// queried once by metrics.NewCollector to fix the `peer` label's presence for the
148	// collector's whole life (T94) — see the metrics package doc for the back-compat rule.
149	func (s *metricsSource) PeerNames() []string {
150		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
151		names := make([]string, len(peers))
152		for i, peer := range peers {
153			names[i] = peer.Name
154		}
155		return names
156	}
157	
158	// Session implements metrics.Source: it reads the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot
159	// (I2) from the engine seam at scrape time. Like FEC it is a direct pass-through of a
160	// snapshot the underlying monitor computes on demand — no per-scrape state is kept here
161	// (the 0->1 edge log is driven by the separate session-monitor poll loop, not by scrapes).
162	func (s *metricsSource) Session() metrics.SessionSnapshot {
163		return s.session.SessionSnapshot()
164	}

4		"errors"

15	// tunDiagnosticInterval is the minimum spacing between actionable EIO diagnostics for a
16	// single TUN. An interface left DOWN (or otherwise wedged) fails EVERY subsequent write, so
17	// without a rate limit a write storm would emit one diagnostic record per dropped packet.
18	// The raw amneziawg-go engine error ("Failed to write packets to TUN device: input/output
19	// error", logged unconditionally via engineLogger's Errorf) still surfaces on every failure
20	// underneath — this decorator adds ONE extra, actionable record per interval on top of that,
21	// it never suppresses the engine's own logging.
22	const tunDiagnosticInterval = 30 * time.Second
23	
24	// diagnosingTUN wraps a tun.Device and, on a Write that fails with EIO, emits ONE rate-limited
25	// actionable ERROR naming the probable cause (the interface's link state and MTU) and the
26	// remedy, alongside the raw error for debugging (D39, I3). Left undiagnosed, a write to a DOWN
27	// wanbond0 surfaces only as the engine's generic "input/output error" — accurate but useless to
28	// an operator who has never hit the failure mode before. Every OTHER tun.Device method passes
29	// straight through via the embedded interface; only Write is intercepted.
30	type diagnosingTUN struct {
31		tun.Device
32		name string
33		log  log.Logger
34		// now and probeState are seams for tests: production wires time.Now and ifState (the real
35		// SIOCGIFFLAGS/SIOCGIFMTU inspection); a test injects a fake clock and a scripted interface
36		// state so it can assert the DOWN/UP wording without a real, privileged network interface.
37		now        func() time.Time
38		probeState func(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error)
39	
40		mu       sync.Mutex
41		lastDiag time.Time
42	}
43	
44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}
49	
50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}
60	
61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}

488		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
489		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
490		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
491		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
492		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.

497		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to

499		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
500		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.
501		// Zero means "not declared" (the synthetic default pace is kept).
502		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
503		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.
504		// "50Mbit" / "1Gbit" / "500kbit" (SI bit/s units; the "bit" suffix may be written
505		// "bps"). Parsed in normalize; a non-positive or unparseable value fails fast.
506		LinkBandwidthRaw string `toml:"link_bandwidth"`
507		// LinkRTT is the OPERATOR-DECLARED baseline RTT of this uplink, parsed from

25	// would let two peers' samples for a same-named path clobber each other's rate
26	// derivation. peer is "" for the single-peer edge/hub and a concentrator's primary,

36	// the previous scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the
37	// Bind's hot path a pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the
38	// only stateful, scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a
39	// (peer,path) reports zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move
40	// backwards (only possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that
41	// interval rather than a spurious negative.

43		provider trafficProvider
44		// session yields the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2), read from the
45		// engine at scrape time. It is separate from provider because the bind stays
46		// WG-unaware — the session signal is sourced by the device layer's engine seam.

53

55	// the instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.

57		total   uint64
58		atNanos int64
59	}
60	
61	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind (per-peer path traffic, FEC,
62	// and resequencer counters) and the engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The
63	// clock is injected so the throughput derivation is deterministic under test.
64	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
65		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[sampleKey]byteSample)}
66	}
67	
68	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
69	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-
70	// (peer,path) last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PeerSnapshots() reads
71	// atomics and each prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock
72	// across a prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
73	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
75		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
76	
77		total := 0
78		for _, peer := range peers {
79			total += len(peer.Paths)
80		}
81	
82		s.mu.Lock()
83		defer s.mu.Unlock()
84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)
85		for _, peer := range peers {
86			for _, t := range peer.Paths {
87				key := sampleKey{peer: peer.Name, path: t.Name}
88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
89				var tput float64
90				if prev, ok := s.last[key]; ok {
91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}

114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
115	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() []metrics.FECSnapshot {

140		for i, peer := range peers {

142		}

2082	// for it. Like tickLivenessFromReceive it only ever TryLocks m.mu, so it can never
2083	// deadlock Close's readersWG.Wait (which runs while Close holds m.mu).
2084	func (m *Multipath) fecTickLoop(period time.Duration, closed <-chan struct{}) {
2085		defer m.readersWG.Done()

2091				return
2092			case <-ticker.C:
2093				m.fecFlushDeadline()
2094			}
2095		}

1	package adaptivefec
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"math"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// MaxShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec
10	// (maxShards) and restated in internal/config (maxFECShards): a coding group
11	// carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It is mirrored
12	// here — with this cross-reference rather than an import — so the controller can
13	// fail-fast on an out-of-field parity bound without coupling to the datapath.
14	const MaxShards = 256
15	
16	// Documented default control-law constants. They are the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH
17	// for the tuning the simulation harness exercises; DefaultConfig assembles them.
18	// Every value is named and justified rather than appearing as a magic number in
19	// the algorithm.
20	const (
21		// DefaultDataShards is K, the fixed number of data shards per coding group the
22		// controller sizes parity for. Chosen to match a typical FEC group.
23		DefaultDataShards = 10
24	
25		// DefaultMaxParity caps M at 100% overhead (M == K): beyond a 1:1 parity ratio
26		// the marginal recovery per byte of overhead is poor, so the loop saturates
27		// there rather than spending unbounded bandwidth under pathological loss.
28		DefaultMaxParity = 10
29	
30		// DefaultAlpha is the EWMA weight of the newest loss sample. 0.3 keeps ~3-4
31		// samples of memory: heavy enough to reject single-sample telemetry noise, light
32		// enough to react to a genuine sustained shift within a few probe intervals.
33		DefaultAlpha = 0.3
34	
35		// DefaultRaiseThreshold is the smoothed-loss level (5%) at/above which the loop
36		// starts adding parity. Below it, occasional loss is cheaper to absorb via
37		// retransmit/latency than to mask with standing redundancy.
38		DefaultRaiseThreshold = 0.05
39	
40		// DefaultLowerThreshold is the smoothed-loss level (2%) at/below which the loop
41		// is eligible to shed parity. The gap to RaiseThreshold is the hysteresis
42		// deadband: loss wandering between 2% and 5% neither raises nor lowers M.
43		DefaultLowerThreshold = 0.02
44	
45		// DefaultSafetyFactor is the headroom over the mean loss the parity is sized to
46		// mask. 1.5 sizes each group to tolerate 1.5x the smoothed loss, covering the
47		// binomial variance by which an individual group exceeds the mean. It applies
48		// only when TargetResidual is unset (the legacy SafetyFactor sizing mode).
49		DefaultSafetyFactor = 1.5
50	
51		// DefaultMaxStep bounds |ΔM| per RateInterval. A single spike can move M by at
52		// most 2 parity shards per interval, so redundancy slews rather than jumps.
53		DefaultMaxStep = 2
54	
55		// DefaultRateInterval is the minimum wall time between two M changes. Combined
56		// with MaxStep it caps the redundancy slew rate at MaxStep shards per 500ms.
57		DefaultRateInterval = 500 * time.Millisecond
58	
59		// DefaultDwell is how long smoothed loss must stay at/below LowerThreshold before
60		// the first decrease. 3s of quiet is required to shed parity, so a brief lull in
61		// a lossy period does not prematurely strip protection (raise fast, lower slow).
62		DefaultDwell = 3 * time.Second
63	)
64	
65	// Config parameterizes the adaptive FEC controller. All fields are validated at
66	// construction (fail-fast): a mis-tuned band or an out-of-field parity bound is
67	// rejected by NewController rather than producing undefined control behavior at
68	// runtime.
69	type Config struct {
70		// DataShards is K, the fixed group size the controller sizes parity for. >= 1.
71		DataShards int
72		// MaxParity is the upper bound on the emitted M. >= 1 and DataShards+MaxParity
73		// <= MaxShards (the Reed-Solomon field limit).
74		MaxParity int
75		// Alpha is the EWMA weight of the newest loss sample, in (0,1]. Smaller = more
76		// smoothing.
77		Alpha float64
78		// RaiseThreshold is the smoothed-loss level at/above which M may rise. In
79		// (LowerThreshold, 1).
80		RaiseThreshold float64
81		// LowerThreshold is the smoothed-loss level at/below which M may fall. In
82		// [0, RaiseThreshold). The gap to RaiseThreshold is the hysteresis deadband.
83		LowerThreshold float64
84		// SafetyFactor is the headroom multiplier on the smoothed loss the parity is
85		// sized to mask. >= 1 in the SafetyFactor sizing mode (TargetResidual unset);
86		// MUST be 0 when TargetResidual is set (the two modes are mutually exclusive).
87		SafetyFactor float64
88		// TargetResidual, when set (> 0), selects the RESIDUAL-SLA sizing mode and is
89		// the PRIMARY parity surface: instead of the bare SafetyFactor multiplier the
90		// controller derives M by inverting the binomial residual model
91		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss), see residual.go) to the smallest
92		// M whose modeled residual is <= TargetResidual, clamped to [0, MaxParity]. It
93		// SUPERSEDES SafetyFactor (which must be 0 when this is set). Must be in (0,1)
94		// when set; 0 selects the legacy SafetyFactor path.
95		TargetResidual float64
96		// MaxStep bounds |ΔM| applied per RateInterval. >= 1.
97		MaxStep int
98		// RateInterval is the minimum time between two M changes. > 0.
99		RateInterval time.Duration
100		// Dwell is the minimum time smoothed loss must stay at/below LowerThreshold
101		// before the first decrease. >= 0.
102		Dwell time.Duration
103	}
104	
105	// DefaultConfig returns a Config populated from the documented default control
106	// constants.
107	func DefaultConfig() Config {
108		return Config{
109			DataShards:     DefaultDataShards,
110			MaxParity:      DefaultMaxParity,
111			Alpha:          DefaultAlpha,
112			RaiseThreshold: DefaultRaiseThreshold,
113			LowerThreshold: DefaultLowerThreshold,
114			SafetyFactor:   DefaultSafetyFactor,
115			MaxStep:        DefaultMaxStep,
116			RateInterval:   DefaultRateInterval,
117			Dwell:          DefaultDwell,
118		}
119	}
120	
121	// Validate enforces the control-law invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned
122	// controller is rejected at construction rather than misbehaving at runtime.
123	func (c Config) Validate() error {
124		if c.DataShards < 1 {
125			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: DataShards (K) must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
126		}
127		if c.MaxParity < 1 {
128			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: MaxParity must be >= 1, got %d", c.MaxParity)
129		}
130		if c.DataShards+c.MaxParity > MaxShards {
131			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: DataShards+MaxParity must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", MaxShards, c.DataShards+c.MaxParity)
132		}
133		if !(c.Alpha > 0 && c.Alpha <= 1) || math.IsNaN(c.Alpha) {
134			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: Alpha must be in (0,1], got %g", c.Alpha)
135		}
136		if math.IsNaN(c.LowerThreshold) || c.LowerThreshold < 0 {
137			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: LowerThreshold must be >= 0, got %g", c.LowerThreshold)
138		}
139		if math.IsNaN(c.RaiseThreshold) || c.RaiseThreshold >= 1 {
140			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: RaiseThreshold must be < 1, got %g", c.RaiseThreshold)
141		}
142		if c.LowerThreshold >= c.RaiseThreshold {
143			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: LowerThreshold (%g) must be < RaiseThreshold (%g) so the hysteresis deadband is non-empty", c.LowerThreshold, c.RaiseThreshold)
144		}
145		if math.IsNaN(c.TargetResidual) {
146			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: TargetResidual must be a number, got NaN")
147		}
148		if c.TargetResidual != 0 {
149			// Residual-SLA sizing mode: TargetResidual governs and SafetyFactor is inert,
150			// so the two must not both be set (mutually exclusive, fail-fast).
151			if c.TargetResidual < 0 || c.TargetResidual >= 1 {
152				return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: TargetResidual must be in (0,1) when set, got %g", c.TargetResidual)
153			}
154			if c.SafetyFactor != 0 {
155				return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: SafetyFactor and TargetResidual are mutually exclusive (TargetResidual is the primary residual-SLA surface); leave SafetyFactor 0 when TargetResidual is set, got SafetyFactor=%g", c.SafetyFactor)
156			}
157		} else if math.IsNaN(c.SafetyFactor) || math.IsInf(c.SafetyFactor, 0) || c.SafetyFactor < 1 {
158			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: SafetyFactor must be a finite value >= 1 (or set TargetResidual instead), got %g", c.SafetyFactor)
159		}
160		if c.MaxStep < 1 {
161			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: MaxStep must be >= 1, got %d", c.MaxStep)
162		}
163		if c.RateInterval <= 0 {
164			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: RateInterval must be > 0, got %s", c.RateInterval)
165		}
166		if c.Dwell < 0 {
167			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: Dwell must be >= 0, got %s", c.Dwell)
168		}
169		return nil
170	}

1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"sync"
7		"syscall"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13	)
14	
15	// tunDiagnosticInterval is the minimum spacing between actionable EIO diagnostics for a
16	// single TUN. An interface left DOWN (or otherwise wedged) fails EVERY subsequent write, so
17	// without a rate limit a write storm would emit one diagnostic record per dropped packet.
18	// The raw amneziawg-go engine error ("Failed to write packets to TUN device: input/output
19	// error", logged unconditionally via engineLogger's Errorf) still surfaces on every failure
20	// underneath — this decorator adds ONE extra, actionable record per interval on top of that,
21	// it never suppresses the engine's own logging.
22	const tunDiagnosticInterval = 30 * time.Second
23	
24	// diagnosingTUN wraps a tun.Device and, on a Write that fails with EIO, emits ONE rate-limited
25	// actionable ERROR naming the probable cause (the interface's link state and MTU) and the
26	// remedy, alongside the raw error for debugging (D39, I3). Left undiagnosed, a write to a DOWN
27	// wanbond0 surfaces only as the engine's generic "input/output error" — accurate but useless to
28	// an operator who has never hit the failure mode before. Every OTHER tun.Device method passes
29	// straight through via the embedded interface; only Write is intercepted.
30	type diagnosingTUN struct {
31		tun.Device
32		name string
33		log  log.Logger
34		// now and probeState are seams for tests: production wires time.Now and ifState (the real
35		// SIOCGIFFLAGS/SIOCGIFMTU inspection); a test injects a fake clock and a scripted interface
36		// state so it can assert the DOWN/UP wording without a real, privileged network interface.
37		now        func() time.Time
38		probeState func(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error)
39	
40		mu       sync.Mutex
41		lastDiag time.Time
42	}
43	
44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}
49	
50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}
60	
61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}

16	// Documented default control-law constants. They are the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH
17	// for the tuning the simulation harness exercises; DefaultConfig assembles them.
18	// Every value is named and justified rather than appearing as a magic number in
19	// the algorithm.
20	const (
21		// DefaultDataShards is K, the fixed number of data shards per coding group the
22		// controller sizes parity for. Chosen to match a typical FEC group.
23		DefaultDataShards = 10
24	
25		// DefaultMaxParity caps M at 100% overhead (M == K): beyond a 1:1 parity ratio
26		// the marginal recovery per byte of overhead is poor, so the loop saturates
27		// there rather than spending unbounded bandwidth under pathological loss.
28		DefaultMaxParity = 10
29	
30		// DefaultAlpha is the EWMA weight of the newest loss sample. 0.3 keeps ~3-4
31		// samples of memory: heavy enough to reject single-sample telemetry noise, light
32		// enough to react to a genuine sustained shift within a few probe intervals.
33		DefaultAlpha = 0.3
34	
35		// DefaultRaiseThreshold is the smoothed-loss level (5%) at/above which the loop
36		// starts adding parity. Below it, occasional loss is cheaper to absorb via
37		// retransmit/latency than to mask with standing redundancy.
38		DefaultRaiseThreshold = 0.05
39	
40		// DefaultLowerThreshold is the smoothed-loss level (2%) at/below which the loop
41		// is eligible to shed parity. The gap to RaiseThreshold is the hysteresis
42		// deadband: loss wandering between 2% and 5% neither raises nor lowers M.
43		DefaultLowerThreshold = 0.02
44	
45		// DefaultSafetyFactor is the headroom over the mean loss the parity is sized to
46		// mask. 1.5 sizes each group to tolerate 1.5x the smoothed loss, covering the
47		// binomial variance by which an individual group exceeds the mean. It applies
48		// only when TargetResidual is unset (the legacy SafetyFactor sizing mode).
49		DefaultSafetyFactor = 1.5
50	
51		// DefaultMaxStep bounds |ΔM| per RateInterval. A single spike can move M by at
52		// most 2 parity shards per interval, so redundancy slews rather than jumps.
53		DefaultMaxStep = 2
54	
55		// DefaultRateInterval is the minimum wall time between two M changes. Combined
56		// with MaxStep it caps the redundancy slew rate at MaxStep shards per 500ms.
57		DefaultRateInterval = 500 * time.Millisecond
58	
59		// DefaultDwell is how long smoothed loss must stay at/below LowerThreshold before
60		// the first decrease. 3s of quiet is required to shed parity, so a brief lull in
61		// a lossy period does not prematurely strip protection (raise fast, lower slow).
62		DefaultDwell = 3 * time.Second
63	)
64	
65	// Config parameterizes the adaptive FEC controller. All fields are validated at
66	// construction (fail-fast): a mis-tuned band or an out-of-field parity bound is
67	// rejected by NewController rather than producing undefined control behavior at
68	// runtime.
69	type Config struct {
70		// DataShards is K, the fixed group size the controller sizes parity for. >= 1.
71		DataShards int
72		// MaxParity is the upper bound on the emitted M. >= 1 and DataShards+MaxParity
73		// <= MaxShards (the Reed-Solomon field limit).
74		MaxParity int
75		// Alpha is the EWMA weight of the newest loss sample, in (0,1]. Smaller = more
76		// smoothing.
77		Alpha float64
78		// RaiseThreshold is the smoothed-loss level at/above which M may rise. In
79		// (LowerThreshold, 1).
80		RaiseThreshold float64
81		// LowerThreshold is the smoothed-loss level at/below which M may fall. In
82		// [0, RaiseThreshold). The gap to RaiseThreshold is the hysteresis deadband.
83		LowerThreshold float64
84		// SafetyFactor is the headroom multiplier on the smoothed loss the parity is
85		// sized to mask. >= 1 in the SafetyFactor sizing mode (TargetResidual unset);
86		// MUST be 0 when TargetResidual is set (the two modes are mutually exclusive).
87		SafetyFactor float64
88		// TargetResidual, when set (> 0), selects the RESIDUAL-SLA sizing mode and is
89		// the PRIMARY parity surface: instead of the bare SafetyFactor multiplier the
90		// controller derives M by inverting the binomial residual model
91		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss), see residual.go) to the smallest
92		// M whose modeled residual is <= TargetResidual, clamped to [0, MaxParity]. It
93		// SUPERSEDES SafetyFactor (which must be 0 when this is set). Must be in (0,1)
94		// when set; 0 selects the legacy SafetyFactor path.
95		TargetResidual float64
96		// MaxStep bounds |ΔM| applied per RateInterval. >= 1.
97		MaxStep int
98		// RateInterval is the minimum time between two M changes. > 0.
99		RateInterval time.Duration
100		// Dwell is the minimum time smoothed loss must stay at/below LowerThreshold
101		// before the first decrease. >= 0.
102		Dwell time.Duration
103	}
104	
105	// DefaultConfig returns a Config populated from the documented default control
106	// constants.
107	func DefaultConfig() Config {
108		return Config{
109			DataShards:     DefaultDataShards,
110			MaxParity:      DefaultMaxParity,
111			Alpha:          DefaultAlpha,
112			RaiseThreshold: DefaultRaiseThreshold,
113			LowerThreshold: DefaultLowerThreshold,
114			SafetyFactor:   DefaultSafetyFactor,
115			MaxStep:        DefaultMaxStep,
116			RateInterval:   DefaultRateInterval,
117			Dwell:          DefaultDwell,
118		}
119	}
120	
121	// Validate enforces the control-law invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned
122	// controller is rejected at construction rather than misbehaving at runtime.
123	func (c Config) Validate() error {
124		if c.DataShards < 1 {
125			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: DataShards (K) must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
126		}
127		if c.MaxParity < 1 {
128			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: MaxParity must be >= 1, got %d", c.MaxParity)
129		}
130		if c.DataShards+c.MaxParity > MaxShards {
131			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: DataShards+MaxParity must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", MaxShards, c.DataShards+c.MaxParity)
132		}
133		if !(c.Alpha > 0 && c.Alpha <= 1) || math.IsNaN(c.Alpha) {
134			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: Alpha must be in (0,1], got %g", c.Alpha)
135		}
136		if math.IsNaN(c.LowerThreshold) || c.LowerThreshold < 0 {
137			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: LowerThreshold must be >= 0, got %g", c.LowerThreshold)
138		}
139		if math.IsNaN(c.RaiseThreshold) || c.RaiseThreshold >= 1 {
140			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: RaiseThreshold must be < 1, got %g", c.RaiseThreshold)
141		}
142		if c.LowerThreshold >= c.RaiseThreshold {
143			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: LowerThreshold (%g) must be < RaiseThreshold (%g) so the hysteresis deadband is non-empty", c.LowerThreshold, c.RaiseThreshold)
144		}
145		if math.IsNaN(c.TargetResidual) {
146			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: TargetResidual must be a number, got NaN")
147		}
148		if c.TargetResidual != 0 {
149			// Residual-SLA sizing mode: TargetResidual governs and SafetyFactor is inert,
150			// so the two must not both be set (mutually exclusive, fail-fast).
151			if c.TargetResidual < 0 || c.TargetResidual >= 1 {
152				return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: TargetResidual must be in (0,1) when set, got %g", c.TargetResidual)
153			}
154			if c.SafetyFactor != 0 {
155				return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: SafetyFactor and TargetResidual are mutually exclusive (TargetResidual is the primary residual-SLA surface); leave SafetyFactor 0 when TargetResidual is set, got SafetyFactor=%g", c.SafetyFactor)
156			}
157		} else if math.IsNaN(c.SafetyFactor) || math.IsInf(c.SafetyFactor, 0) || c.SafetyFactor < 1 {
158			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: SafetyFactor must be a finite value >= 1 (or set TargetResidual instead), got %g", c.SafetyFactor)
159		}
160		if c.MaxStep < 1 {
161			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: MaxStep must be >= 1, got %d", c.MaxStep)
162		}
163		if c.RateInterval <= 0 {
164			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: RateInterval must be > 0, got %s", c.RateInterval)
165		}
166		if c.Dwell < 0 {
167			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: Dwell must be >= 0, got %s", c.Dwell)
168		}
169		return nil
170	}

1	package device
